title: Creating New Columns with mutate() and transmute()
description: Add new variables or modify existing ones using mutate().

slides.title: mutate(): Add New Columns
slides.content:
`mutate()` adds new columns at the end of your data frame. You can refer to existing columns.

```r
mtcars %>%
  mutate(kmpl = mpg * 0.425)  # convert to km per liter
```

You can create multiple columns in one `mutate()`, and newly created columns can be used immediately in the same call.

slides.title: mutate() Examples
slides.codeSnippet:
library(dplyr)
mtcars %>%
  mutate(kmpl = mpg * 0.425,
         wt_kg = wt * 453.592 / 1000,  # convert to kg
         efficiency = kmpl / wt_kg) %>%
  select(mpg, kmpl, wt, wt_kg, efficiency) %>%
  head()

quizData.question: What does `mtcars %>% mutate(cyl2 = cyl * 2, gear2 = cyl2 + gear)` do?

options.text: It creates cyl2 and then uses it to create gear2
Error because cyl2 doesn't exist when gear2 is created

quizData.explanation: mutate creates columns sequentially, so gear2 can use cyl2.

slides.title: transmute(): Keep Only New Columns
slides.content:
`transmute()` works like `mutate()` but drops all existing columns, keeping only the new ones.

```r
mtcars %>% transmute(kmpl = mpg * 0.425, hp_per_cyl = hp / cyl)
```

slides.title: transmute() Example
slides.codeSnippet:
library(dplyr)
mtcars %>% transmute(disp_l = disp / 61.02, hp_per_cyl = hp / cyl) %>% head()

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content: Use `if_else()` or `case_when()` inside `mutate()` for conditional transformations.
